question_text: Show that the relation R in the set Z of integers given by R = {(a, b) : 2 divides a-b} is an equivalence relation.
question_html: Show that the relation R in the set Z of integers given by R = {(a, b) : 2 divides a-b} is an equivalence relation.
solution_text: R is reflexive , as 2 divide a - a = 0 Let ((a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R then(a-b) is divided by 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b-a) is divided by 2. Therefore (b,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R and hence R is symmetric. Let a,b,c {tex}\in{/tex} Z such that(a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R Then, a - b and b - c is divided by 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - b) is even and ( b-c) is even {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - b +b - c is even, as sum of two even numbers is even {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - c) is even {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - c is divided by 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a , c) {tex}\in{/tex} R Hence, R is transitive. Therefore, R is an equivalence relation.
solution_html:
R is reflexive , as 2 divide a - a = 0
Let ((a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R then(a-b) is divided by 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b-a) is divided by 2. Therefore (b,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R and hence R is symmetric.
Let a,b,c {tex}\in{/tex} Z such that(a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Then, a - b and b - c is divided by 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - b) is even and ( b-c) is even
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - b +b - c is even, as sum of two even numbers is even
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - c) is even
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - c is divided by 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a , c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Hence, R is transitive.

Therefore, R is an equivalence relation.

question_text: Let A be the set of all 50 students of Class X in a school. Let f : {tex}A \rightarrow N{/tex} be function defined by f(x) = roll number of the student x. Show that f is one-one but not onto.
question_html: Let A be the set of all 50 students of Class X in a school. Let f : {tex}A \rightarrow N{/tex} be function defined by f(x) = roll number of the student x. Show that f is one-one but not onto.
solution_text: No two different students of the class can have the same roll number. Therefore, f must be one-one. We can assume without any loss of generality that roll numbers of students are from 1 to 50. This implies that 51 and higher natural numbers in N cannot be the roll numbers of any student in the class. So, 51 and higher natural numbers can not be the images of any element of A under f. Hence, f is not onto.
solution_html:
No two different students of the class can have the same roll number.
Therefore, f must be one-one.
We can assume without any loss of generality that roll numbers of students are from 1 to 50.
This implies that 51 and higher natural numbers in N cannot be the roll numbers of any student in the class. 
So, 51 and higher natural numbers can not be the images of any element of A under f. Hence, f is not onto.

Therefore, no odd natural number in N (co-domain) has its pre-image in domain. So, f is not onto.

solution_text: It is given that {tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1,x = 1,2}\\ {x - 1,x \ge 2} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} Clearly, f (1) = f (2) = 1 i.e. 1 and 2 have the same image. So, f: N {tex}\to {/tex} N is a many-one function. Let y be an arbitrary element in N (Co-domain). Then, f(x) = y {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x - 1 = y {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x = y + 1 Clearly, y + 1 {tex}\in{/tex} N (domain) for all y e N (Co-domain). Thus, for each y {tex}\in{/tex} N (co-domain) there exists y + 1 {tex}\in{/tex} N (domain) such that f(y + 1 ) = y + 1 - 1 = y. So, f: N {tex}\to {/tex} N is an onto function.
solution_html:
It is given that
{tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1,x = 1,2}\\ {x - 1,x \ge 2} \end{array}} \right.{/tex}
Clearly, f (1) = f (2) = 1 i.e. 1 and 2 have the same image.
So, f: N {tex}\to {/tex}N is a many-one function.
Let y be an arbitrary element in N (Co-domain). Then,
f(x) = y {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x - 1 = y {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}x = y + 1
Clearly, y + 1 {tex}\in{/tex}N (domain) for all y e N (Co-domain). Thus, for each y {tex}\in{/tex} N (co-domain) there exists y + 1 {tex}\in{/tex} N (domain) such that f(y + 1 ) = y + 1 - 1 = y.
So, f: N {tex}\to {/tex}N is an onto function.

question_text: Show that f : N {tex} \to{/tex} N, given by {tex} f ( x ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { x + 1 , } & { \text { if } x \text { is odd } } \\ { x - 1 , } & { \text { if } x \text { is even } } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is both one-one and onto.
question_html: Show that f : N {tex} \to{/tex} N, given by {tex} f ( x ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { x + 1 , } & { \text { if } x \text { is odd } } \\ { x - 1 , } & { \text { if } x \text { is even } } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is both one-one and onto.
solution_html:
Given function is f: N {tex} \to{/tex} N such that
{tex} f ( x ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { x + 1 , } & { \text { if } x \text { is odd } } \\ { x - 1 , } & { \text { if } x \text { is even } } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
One-One function
Case I: When x1 and x2 are odd.
Then, {tex}f(x1) = f(x2){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x_1 -1 = x_2 -1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x_1 = x_2{/tex}
Case II: When x1 and x2 are even.
Then, {tex}f(x_1) = f(x_2){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x_1 + 1 = x_2 + 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x_1 = x_2{/tex}
Thus, in both cases,
{tex}f(x_1)= f(x_2){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}x_1 = x_2{/tex}
Case III: When x1 is odd and x2 is even.
Then, x1 {tex} \ne{/tex} x2
Also, f(x1) is even and f(x2) is odd.
So, f(x1) {tex} \ne{/tex} f(x2)
Thus, {tex}x_1{/tex} {tex} \ne{/tex} {tex}x_2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f(x_1){/tex} {tex} \ne{/tex} {tex}f(x_2){/tex}
Case IV: When x1 is even and x2 is odd.
Then, x1 {tex} \ne{/tex} x2
Also, f(x1) is odd and f(x2) is even.
So, f(x1) {tex} \ne{/tex} f(x2)
Thus, x1 {tex} \ne{/tex} x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) {tex}\ne{/tex} f(x2)
Hence, from cases I, II, III and IV we can obsere that, f(x) is a one-one function.
Onto function
Let y{tex}\in{/tex}N ( co-domain) be any arbitrary number.
If y is odd, then there exists an even number {tex}y+ 1{/tex} {tex} \in{/tex} N (domain) such that {tex}f(y+ 1) = (y+ 1 ) - 1 = y.{/tex}
If y is even, then there exists an odd number {tex}y - 1{/tex} {tex} \in{/tex} N (domain) such that {tex}f(y-1) = (y-1) + 1 = y{/tex}
Thus, every element in N ( codomain) has a pre-image in N (domain).
Therefore, f(x) is an onto function. Hence, the function f(x) is bijective.

question_text: Show that an onto function f : {1, 2, 3} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {1, 2, 3} is always one-one.
question_html: Show that an onto function f : {1, 2, 3} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {1, 2, 3} is always one-one.
solution_text: Suppose f is not one-one. Then there exists two elements, say 1 and 2 in the domain whose image in the co-domain is the same. Also, the image of 3 under f can be only one element. Therefore, the range set can have at the most two elements of the co-domain {1, 2, 3}, showing that f is not onto, a contradiction. Hence, f must be one-one.

question_text: Find the number of all one-one functions from set A = {1, 2, 3} to itself.
question_html: Find the number of all one-one functions from set A = {1, 2, 3} to itself.
solution_text: One-one function from {1, 2, 3} to itself is simply a permutation on three symbols 1, 2, 3. Therefore, a total number of one-one maps from {1, 2, 3} to itself is the same as the total number of permutations on three symbols 1, 2, 3 which is 3! = 6

question_text: Let A = {1, 2, 3}. Then show that the number of relations containing (1, 2) and (2, 3) which are reflexive and transitive but not symmetric is three.
question_html: Let A = {1, 2, 3}. Then show that the number of relations containing (1, 2) and (2, 3) which are reflexive and transitive but not symmetric is three.
solution_text: The smallest relation R 1 containing (1, 2) and (2, 3) which is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric is {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)}. Now, if we add the pair (2, 1) to R 1 to get R 2 , then the relation R 2 will be reflexive, transitive but not symmetric. Similarly, we can obtain R 3 by adding (3, 2) to R 1 to get the desired relation. However, we can not add the pairs (2, 1), (3, 2) and (3, 1) to R 1 at a time, as by doing so the relation will become symmetric, which is not required. At a time, we can only add two pairs out of the remaining three viz. (2,1), (3,2) and (3,1). This can be done in three ways adding (2,1) and (3,2) or (2,1) and (3,1) or (3,2) and (3,1) Thus, the total number of desired relations is three.
solution_html:
The smallest relation R1 containing (1, 2) and (2, 3) which is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric is {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)}.
Now, if we add the pair (2, 1) to R1 to get R2, then the relation R2 will be reflexive, transitive but not symmetric.
Similarly, we can obtain R3 by adding (3, 2) to R1 to get the desired relation.
However, we can not add the pairs (2, 1), (3, 2) and (3, 1) to R1 at a time, as by doing so the relation will become symmetric, which is not required. At a time, we can only add two pairs out of the remaining three viz. (2,1), (3,2) and (3,1). This can be done in three ways
adding (2,1) and (3,2)
or  (2,1) and (3,1)
or  (3,2) and (3,1)
Thus, the total number of desired relations is three.

question_text: Show that the number of equivalence relations on the set {1, 2, 3} containing (1, 2) and (2, 1) is two.
question_html: Show that the number of equivalence relations on the set {1, 2, 3} containing (1, 2) and (2, 1) is two.
solution_text: The smallest equivalence relation R 1 containing (1, 2) and (2, 1) is {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 1)}. Now we are left with only 4 pairs namely (2, 3), (3, 2), (1, 3) and (3, 1). If we add anyone, say (2, 3) to R 1 , then for symmetry, we must add (3, 2) this loses transitivity. To maintain transitivity along with symmetry, we are forced to add (1, 3) and (3, 1). Thus, the only equivalence relation bigger than R 1 is the universal relation. This shows that the total number of equivalence relations containing (1, 2) and (2, 1) is two.
solution_html:
The smallest equivalence relation R1 containing (1, 2) and (2, 1) is {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 1)}.
Now we are left with only 4 pairs namely (2, 3), (3, 2), (1, 3) and (3, 1).
If we add anyone, say (2, 3) to R1, then for symmetry, we must add (3, 2) this loses transitivity.
To maintain transitivity along with symmetry, we are forced to add (1, 3) and (3, 1).
Thus, the only equivalence relation bigger than R1 is the universal relation.
This shows that the total number of equivalence relations containing (1, 2) and (2, 1) is two.

solution_text: It is given that Relation R in the set Z of all integers is defined as R = {(x, y) : x – y is an integer} Now, for every x {tex}\in{/tex} Z, (x, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R, as x - x = 0 is an integer. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is reflexive. Next, for every x, y {tex}\in{/tex} Z if (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R, then x - y is an integer. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -(x - y) is also an integer. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - x) is an integer. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - x) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is symmetric. Further, Take (x, y) ,(y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R where x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} R, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) and (y - z) are integers. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - z) = (x - y) + (y - x) is an integer. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive. Therefore, R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

Now, for every x {tex}\in{/tex} Z, (x, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R, as x - x = 0 is an integer.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is reflexive.
Next, for every x, y {tex}\in{/tex} Z if (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R, then x - y is an integer.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -(x - y) is also an integer.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - x) is an integer.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - x) {tex}\in{/tex} R

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) and (y - z) are integers.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - z) = (x - y) + (y - x) is an integer.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive.
Therefore, R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

question_text: Show that the relation R in the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} given by R = {(a, b) : |a - b| is even}, is an equivalence relation. Show that all the elements of {1, 3, 5} are related to each other and all the elements of {2, 4} are related to each other. But no element of {1, 3, 5} is related to any element of {2, 4}.
question_html: Show that the relation R in the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} given by R = {(a, b) : |a - b| is even}, is an equivalence relation. Show that all the elements of {1, 3, 5} are related to each other and all the elements of {2, 4} are related to each other. But no element of {1, 3, 5} is related to any element of {2, 4}.
solution_text: A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and R = {(a, b) : |a - b| is even}, then R = {(1, 3), (1, 5), (3, 5), (2, 4)} For (a, a), |a - a| = 0 which is even. {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive. If |a - b| is even, then |b - a| is also even. {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric. Now, if |a - b| and |b - c| is even then |a - b + b - c| is even {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a - c| is also even. {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive. Therefore, R is an equivalence relation. Elements of {1, 3, 5} are related to each other. Since |1 - 3| = 2, |3 - 5| = 2, |1 - 5| = 4 all are even numbers {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Elements of {1, 3, 5} are related to each other. Similarly elements of (2, 4) are related to each other. Since |2 - 4| = 2 an even number, then no element of the set {1, 3, 5} is related to any element of (2, 4). Hence no element of {1, 3, 5} is related to any element of {2, 4}.
solution_html:
A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and R = {(a, b) : |a - b| is even}, then R = {(1, 3), (1, 5), (3, 5), (2, 4)}

  1. For (a, a), |a - a| = 0 which is even. {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive.
    If |a - b| is even, then |b - a| is also even. {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric.
    Now, if |a - b| and |b - c| is even then |a - b + b - c| is even
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a - c| is also even. {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive.
    Therefore, R is an equivalence relation.
  2. Elements of {1, 3, 5} are related to each other.
    Since |1 - 3| = 2, |3 - 5| = 2, |1 - 5| = 4 all are even numbers
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Elements of {1, 3, 5} are related to each other.
    Similarly elements of (2, 4) are related to each other.
    Since |2 - 4| = 2 an even number, then no element of the set {1, 3, 5} is related to any element of (2, 4).
    Hence no element of {1, 3, 5} is related to any element of {2, 4}.

question_text: Show the relation R in the set A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12}, given by R = {(a, b) : |a – b| is a multiple of 4} is an equivalence relation. Find the set of all elements related to 1 in each case.
question_html:
Show the relation R in the set A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12}, given by
R = {(a, b) : |a – b| is a multiple of 4} is an equivalence relation.
Find the set of all elements related to 1 in each case.
solution_text: The relation R on the set A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12}, is given by R = {(a, b) : |a – b| is a multiple of 4} For any element a {tex}\in{/tex} A, we have (a,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R as |a-a|=0 is a multiple of 4. Therefore, R is reflexive. Now, Let (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a – b| is a multiple of 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |b – a| = |a – b| is a multiple of 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R Therefore, R is symmetric. Finally, Let (a,b), (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a – b| is a multiple of 4 and |b - c| is a multiple of 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a-b {/tex} is a multiple of 4 and {tex}b-c{/tex} is a multiple of 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a-c=a-b+b-c{/tex} , is a multiple of 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|a-c|{/tex} is a multiple of 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R Therefore, R is transitive. Hence, R is an equivalence relation. The set of elements related to 1 is {1,5,9} |1-1| = 0 is multiple of 4 |5-1| = 4 is multiple of 4 |9-1| = 8 is multiple of 4.
solution_html:
The relation R on the set A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12}, is given by
R = {(a, b) : |a – b| is a multiple of 4}
For any element a {tex}\in{/tex} A, we have (a,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R as |a-a|=0 is a multiple of 4.
Therefore, R is reflexive.
Now, Let (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a – b| is a multiple of 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |b – a| = |a – b| is a multiple of 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Therefore, R is symmetric.
Finally, Let (a,b), (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a – b| is a multiple of 4 and |b - c| is a multiple of 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a-b {/tex}  is a multiple of 4 and {tex}b-c{/tex}  is a multiple of 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a-c=a-b+b-c{/tex},  is a multiple of 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|a-c|{/tex}  is a multiple of 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Therefore, R is transitive.
Hence, R is an equivalence relation.
The set of elements related to 1 is {1,5,9}
|1-1| = 0 is multiple of 4
|5-1| = 4 is multiple of 4
|9-1| = 8 is multiple of 4.

question_text: Show the relation R in the set A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12}, given by R = {(a, b) : a = b}, is an equivalence relation. Find the set of all elements related to 1 in each case.
question_html: Show the relation R in the set A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex}12}, given by R = {(a, b) : a = b}, is an equivalence relation. Find the set of all elements related to 1 in each case.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A=\{x \in Z : 0 \leq x \leq 12\}{/tex} be a set and R = {(a, b) : a = b} be a relation on A Now, Reflexivity: Let a {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is reflexive Symmetric: Let a, b, {tex}\in{/tex} A and (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is symmetric Transitive: Let a, b & c {tex}\in{/tex} A and let (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b and b = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive Since R is being reflexive, symmetric and transitive, so R is an equivalence relation. Also we need to find the set of all elements related to 1. Since the relation is given by, R = {(a, b): a = b}, and 1 is an element of A. R = {(1, 1): 1 = 1} Thus, the set of all elements related to 1 is {1}.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A=\{x \in Z : 0 \leq x \leq 12\}{/tex}be a set and
R = {(a, b) : a = b} be a relation on A
Now, 
Reflexivity: Let a {tex}\in{/tex} A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is reflexive
Symmetric: Let a, b, {tex}\in{/tex} A and (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is symmetric
Transitive: Let a, b & c {tex}\in{/tex} A
and let (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b and b = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive
Since R is being reflexive, symmetric and transitive, so R is an equivalence relation.
Also we need to find the set of all elements related to 1.
Since the relation is given by, R = {(a, b): a = b}, and 1 is an element of A.
R = {(1, 1): 1 = 1}
Thus, the set of all elements related to 1 is {1}.

question_text: Give an example of a relation which is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive.
question_html: Give an example of a relation which is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive.
solution_text: Let A = {3,4,5} Define a relation R on A as R = {(3,4), (4,3)} Relation R is not reflexive as (3,3), (4,4) ,(5,5) {tex}\notin{/tex} R. Now, as (3,4) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (4,3) {tex}\in{/tex} R, R is symmetric. Further, (3,4),(4,3) {tex}\in{/tex} R, but (3,3) {tex}\notin{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is not transitive. Therefore, relation R is symmetric but not reflexive or transitive.

solution_text: Let us take A = {2,4,6} Define a relation R on A as: A = {(2,2), (4,4), (6,6), (2,4), (4,2), (4,6), (6,4)} Relation of R is reflexive as for every a {tex}\in{/tex} A, (a,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2,2), (4,4), (6,6) {tex}\in{/tex} R, Relation R is symmetric as (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R for all a ,b {tex}\in{/tex} R And Relation R is not transitive as (2,4), (4,6) {tex}\in{/tex} R, but (2,6) {tex}\notin{/tex} R Therefore, relation R is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.
solution_html:

Define a relation R on A as:
A = {(2,2), (4,4), (6,6), (2,4), (4,2), (4,6), (6,4)}
Relation of R is reflexive as for every a {tex}\in{/tex} A,
(a,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R

And Relation R is not transitive as (2,4), (4,6) {tex}\in{/tex} R,
but (2,6) {tex}\notin{/tex} R
Therefore, relation R is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.

question_text: Show that the relation R in the set A of points in a plane given by R = {(P, Q) : distance of the point P from the origin is same as the distance of the point Q from the origin}, is an equivalence relation. Further, show that the set of all points related to a point P {tex}\ne{/tex} (0, 0) is the circle passing through P with origin as centre.
question_html: Show that the relation R in the set A of points in a plane given by R = {(P, Q) : distance of the point P from the origin is same as the distance of the point Q from the origin}, is an equivalence relation. Further, show that the set of all points related to a point P {tex}\ne{/tex} (0, 0) is the circle passing through P with origin as centre.
solution_text: Part I : R = {(P, Q): distance of the point P from the origin is the same as the distance of the point Q from the origin} Let P(x 1 , y 1 ) and Q(x 2 , y 2 ) and O (0, 0). {tex}\therefore{/tex} OP = OQ {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {x_1^2 + y_1^2} = \sqrt {x_2^2 + y_2^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x_1^2 + y_1^2 = x_2^2 + y_2^2{/tex} Now, For (P, P), OP = OP {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive. Also OP = OQ and OQ = OP {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (P, Q) = (Q, P) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric. Also OP = OQ and OQ = OR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OP = OQ {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive. Therefore, R is an equivalent relation. Part II : As {tex}x_1^2 + y_1^2 = x_2^2 + y_2^2 = {r^2}{/tex} (let) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = r 2 which represents a circle with centre (0, 0) and radius r.
solution_html:
Part I: R = {(P, Q): distance of the point P from the origin is the same as the distance of the point Q from the origin}
Let P(x1, y1) and Q(x2, y2) and O (0, 0).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OP = OQ {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {x_1^2 + y_1^2} = \sqrt {x_2^2 + y_2^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x_1^2 + y_1^2 = x_2^2 + y_2^2{/tex}
Now, For (P, P), OP = OP {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive.
Also OP = OQ and OQ = OP {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (P, Q) = (Q, P) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric.
Also OP = OQ and OQ = OR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OP = OQ {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive.
Therefore, R is an equivalent relation.
Part II: As {tex}x_1^2 + y_1^2 = x_2^2 + y_2^2 = {r^2}{/tex} (let) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = r2 which represents a circle with centre (0, 0) and radius r.

question_text: Show that the relation R defined in the set A of all triangles as R = {(T 1 , T 2 ) : T 1 is similar to T 2 }, is equivalence relation. Consider three right angle triangles T 1 with sides 3, 4, 5, T 2 with sides 5, 12, 13 and T 3 with sides 6, 8, 10. Which triangles among T 1 , T 2 and T 3 are related?
question_html: Show that the relation R defined in the set A of all triangles as R = {(T1, T2) : T1 is similar to T2}, is equivalence relation. Consider three right angle triangles T1 with sides 3, 4, 5, T2 with sides 5, 12, 13 and T3 with sides 6, 8, 10. Which triangles among T1, T2 and T3 are related?
solution_text: Part I : R = {(T 1 , T 2 ): T 1 is similar to T 2 } and T 1 , T 2 are triangles. We know that each triangle similar to itself and thus (T 1 , T 1 ) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive. Also if two triangles are similar, then T 1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T 1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric. Again, if T 1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T 2 and T 2 {tex}\cong{/tex} T 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} then T 1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive. Therefore, R is an equivalent relation. Part II : It is given that T 1 , T 2 and T 3 are right angled triangles. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T 1 with sides 3, 4, 5, T 2 with sides 5, 12, 13 and T 3 with sides 6, 8, 10 Since, two triangles are similar if corresponding sides are proportional. Therefore, {tex}\frac{3}{6} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{5}{{10}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, T 1 and T 3 are related.
solution_html:
Part I: R = {(T1, T2): T1 is similar to T2} and T1, T2 are triangles.
We know that each triangle similar to itself and thus (T1, T1){tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive.
Also if two triangles are similar, then T1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric.
Again, if T1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T2 and T2 {tex}\cong{/tex} T3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} then T1 {tex}\cong{/tex} T3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive.
Therefore, R is an equivalent relation.
Part II: It is given that T1, T2 and T3 are right angled triangles.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T1 with sides 3, 4, 5, T2 with sides 5, 12, 13 and T3 with sides 6, 8, 10
Since, two triangles are similar if corresponding sides are proportional.
Therefore, {tex}\frac{3}{6} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{5}{{10}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, T1 and T3 are related.

question_text: Show that the relation R defined in the set A of all polygons as R = {(P 1 , P 2 ) : P 1 and P 2 have same number of sides}, is an equivalence relation. What is the set of all elements in A related to the right angle triangle T with sides 3, 4, and 5?
question_html: Show that the relation R defined in the set A of all polygons as R = {(P1, P2) : P1 and P2 have same number of sides}, is an equivalence relation. What is the set of all elements in A related to the right angle triangle T with sides 3, 4, and 5?
solution_text: Part I : R = {(P 1 , P 2 ): P 1 and P 2 have same number of sides} Consider the element (P 1 , P 1 ), it shows P 1 and P 1 have same number of sides. Therefore, R is reflexive. If (P 1 , P 2 ) {tex}\in{/tex} R then P 1 and P 2 have same no.of pages .Then, P 2 and P 1 have same no. of pages so (P 2 , P 1 ) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric. If (P 1 , P 2 ) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (P 2 , P 3 ) {tex}\in{/tex} R then P 1 and P 2 have same no. of pages and P 2 and P 3 have same no. of pages .This implies P 1 and P 3 have same no. of pages and hence (P 1 , P 3 ) {tex}\in{/tex} R ,therefore, R is transitive. Therefore, R is an equivalent relation. Part II : Since the relation considers only the number of sides, therefore, all the triangles are similar to the given triangle.
solution_html:
Part I: R = {(P1, P2): P1 and P2 have same number of sides}

  1. Consider the element (P1, P1), it shows P1 and P1 have same number of sides. Therefore, R is reflexive.
  2. If (P1, P2) {tex}\in{/tex} R then P1 and P2 have same no.of pages .Then, P2 and P1 have same no. of pages so (P2, P1) {tex}\in{/tex} R
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric.
  3. If (P1, P2) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (P2, P3) {tex}\in{/tex} R then P1 and P2 have same no. of pages and P2 and P3 have same no. of pages .This implies P1 and P3 have same no. of pages and hence (P1, P3) {tex}\in{/tex} R ,therefore, R is transitive.

Therefore, R is an equivalent relation.

Part II: Since the relation considers only the number of sides, therefore, all the triangles are similar to the given triangle.

question_html: Let A and B be sets. Show that f : A {tex}\times {/tex} B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B {tex}\times {/tex} A such that f(a, b) = (b, a) is a bijective function.
solution_text: Injectivity: Let (a 1 , b 1 ) and (a 2 , b 2 ) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B such that f(a 1 , b 1 ) = f(a 2 , b 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b 1 , a 1 ) = (b 2 , a 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b 1 = b 2 and a 1 = a 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a 1 , b 1 ) = (a 2 , b 2 ) for all (a 1 , b 1 ), (a 2 , b 2 ) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B So, f is injective. Surjectivity: Let (b, a) be an arbitrary element of B {tex}\times{/tex} A. Then b {tex}\in{/tex} B and a {tex}\in{/tex} A. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B Thus, for all (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} A, there exists (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B such that f(a, b) = (b, a) So, f : A {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} A is an onto function, therefore f is bijective.
solution_html:
Injectivity: Let (a1, b1) and (a2, b2) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B such that f(a1, b1) = f(a2, b2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b1, a1) = (b2, a2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b1 = b2 and a1 = a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a1, b1) = (a2, b2) for all (a1, b1), (a2, b2) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B
So, f is injective.
Surjectivity: Let (b, a) be an arbitrary element of B {tex}\times{/tex} A. Then b {tex}\in{/tex} B and a {tex}\in{/tex} A.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B
Thus, for all (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} A, there exists (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B such that f(a, b) = (b, a)
So, f : A {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} A is an onto function, therefore f is bijective.

question_text: Let A = R - {3} and B = R - {1}. Consider the function f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B defined by {tex}f(x) = \left( {\frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}} \right){/tex} Is f one-one and onto? Justify your answer.
question_html: Let A = R - {3} and B = R - {1}. Consider the function f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B defined by {tex}f(x) = \left( {\frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}} \right){/tex} Is f one-one and onto? Justify your answer.
solution_text: A = R - {3} and B = R - {1} and {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}{/tex} Let x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} A, then {tex}f({x_1}) = \frac{{{x_1} - 2}}{{{x_1} - 3}}{/tex} and {tex}f({x_2}) = \frac{{{x_2} - 2}}{{{x_2} - 3}}{/tex} Now, for f(x 1 ) = f(x 2 ) {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{x_1} - 2}}{{{x_1} - 3}} = \frac{{{x_2} - 2}}{{{x_2} - 3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 1 - 2)(x 2 - 3) = (x 2 - 2)(x 1 - 3) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 x 2 - 3x 1 - 2x 2 +6 = x 1 x 2 - 2x 1 - 3x 2 + 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3x 1 - 2x 2 = -2x 1 - 3x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is one-one function. Now, {tex}y = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x - 3) = x - 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 3y = x - 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y - 1) = 3y - 2 {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore f\left( {\frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}}} \right) = \frac{{\frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}} - 2}}{{\frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}} - 3}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{3y - 2 - 2y + 2}}{{3y - 2 - 3y + 3}} = y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = y Therefore, f is an onto function.
solution_html:
A = R - {3} and B = R - {1} and {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}{/tex}
Let x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} A, then {tex}f({x_1}) = \frac{{{x_1} - 2}}{{{x_1} - 3}}{/tex} and {tex}f({x_2}) = \frac{{{x_2} - 2}}{{{x_2} - 3}}{/tex}
Now, for f(x1) = f(x2)
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{x_1} - 2}}{{{x_1} - 3}} = \frac{{{x_2} - 2}}{{{x_2} - 3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x1 - 2)(x2 - 3) = (x2 - 2)(x1 - 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1x2 - 3x1 - 2x2 +6 = x1x2 - 2x1 - 3x2 + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3x1 - 2x2 = -2x1 - 3x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is one-one function.
Now, {tex}y = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x - 3) = x - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 3y = x - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y - 1) = 3y - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f\left( {\frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}}} \right) = \frac{{\frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}} - 2}}{{\frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}} - 3}}{/tex}{tex} = \frac{{3y - 2 - 2y + 2}}{{3y - 2 - 3y + 3}} = y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = y
Therefore, f is an onto function.

question_html: Show that the function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : -1 < x < 1} defined by {tex}f(x) = \frac{x}{{1 + |x|}}{/tex}, x {tex}\in{/tex} R is one-one and onto function.
solution_text: f is one-one: For any x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R, we have f(x) : f(y) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{{1 + |x|}} = \frac{y}{{|y| + 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy + x = xy + y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Therefore, f is one-one function. If f is one-one, let y = R – {1}, then f(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{{x + 1}} = y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{y}{{1 - y}}{/tex} It is clear that x {tex}\in{/tex} R for all y = R – {1}, also x {tex}\ne{/tex} =-1 Because x = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{{1 - y}} = - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -1 + y which is not possible. Thus for each R – {1} there exists {tex}x = \frac{y}{{1 - y}} \in{/tex} R – {1} such that {tex}f(x) = \frac{x}{{x + 1}} = \frac{{\frac{y}{{1 - y}}}}{{\frac{y}{{1 - y}} + 1}} = y{/tex} Therefore f is onto function.
solution_html:
f is one-one: For any x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R, we have f(x) : f(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{{1 + |x|}} = \frac{y}{{|y| + 1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy + x = xy + y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y
Therefore, f is one-one function.
If f is one-one, let y = R – {1}, then f(x) = y
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{{x + 1}} = y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{y}{{1 - y}}{/tex}
It is clear that x {tex}\in{/tex} R for all y = R – {1}, also x {tex}\ne{/tex}=-1
Because x = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{{1 - y}} = - 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -1 + y which is not possible.
Thus for each R – {1} there exists {tex}x = \frac{y}{{1 - y}} \in{/tex} R – {1} such that
{tex}f(x) = \frac{x}{{x + 1}} = \frac{{\frac{y}{{1 - y}}}}{{\frac{y}{{1 - y}} + 1}} = y{/tex}
Therefore f is onto function.

solution_text: When x = -1 then f(x) = 1 2 + 1 = 2 and {tex}g(x) = 2\left| { - 1 - \frac{1}{2}} \right| - 1 = 2{/tex} At x = 0, f(0) = 0 and {tex}g(0) = 2\left| { - \frac{1}{2}} \right| - 1 = 2 \times \frac{1}{2} - 1 = 0{/tex} At x = 1, f(1) = 1 2 - 1 = 0 and {tex}g(1) = 2\left| {1 - \frac{1}{2}} \right| - 1 = 2 \times \frac{1}{2} - 1 = 0{/tex} At x = 2, f(2) = 2 2 - 2 = 2 and {tex}g(2) = 2\left| {2 - \frac{1}{2}} \right| - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2{/tex} Thus for each a {tex}\in{/tex} A, f(a) = g(a) Therefore, f and g are equal function.
